subtopic: SDG Progress and Financing for Development
title: 10 Years of SDGs: Progress and Related Reports
focus: India's and global progress on SDGs after 10 years, key achievements, and major global/national reports tracking SDG implementation.
rationale: This section covers the foundational knowledge of SDGs, recent index rankings (India breaking into the top 100), and crucial reports released by UN bodies and NITI Aayog, which are highly testable in UPSC prelims.
sectionExcerpt: UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDG) completed 10 years of adoption since September 2015. In 2015, all United Nations countries adopted 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development. Sets out 17 SDGs and 169 targets. For the first time, India ranks among top 100 in SDG Index at 99 in 2025 with a score of 67 out of 100. Sustainable Development Report (SDR) 2025 Released by: SDG Transformation Centre, under Sustainable Development Solutions Network (SDSN). Sustainable Development Goals Report (SDGR) 2025 Prepared by: UN DESA. SDG India Index: Released by: NITI Aayog. Voluntary National Review (VNR) Report 2025: Released by: NITI Aayog.
evidenceBullets: SDGs were adopted in 2015 (17 goals, 169 targets), succeeding the Millennium Development Goals (MDGs).
India ranked 99th (score 67/100) in the 10th Sustainable Development Report (SDR) 2025, entering the top 100 for the first time.
The Sustainable Development Report (SDR) is released by the UN Sustainable Development Solutions Network (SDSN).
The Sustainable Development Goals Report (SDGR) is the only official UN report monitoring global progress, prepared by UN DESA.
NITI Aayog releases the SDG India Index, District SDG Index for NER, and India's Voluntary National Review (VNR) Report.
MoSPI launched the National Indicator Framework (NIF) Progress Report 2025.

subtopic: SDG Progress and Financing for Development
title: Financing for Sustainable Development and the Sevilla Commitment
focus: The outcomes of the 4th International Conference on Financing for Development (FFD4) and the new financing mechanisms introduced under the Sevilla Commitment.
rationale: The Sevilla Commitment introduces several new economic and environmental financing mechanisms (Debt swaps, SCALED, Solidarity Levies) which are prime targets for UPSC economy and environment questions.
sectionExcerpt: Recently, the 4th International Conference on Financing for Development (FFD4) adopted the final outcome document Compromiso de Sevilla, also known as Sevilla Commitment, to address the SDG financing gap in developing countries. Adopted by consensus, lays out a path to close the $4 trillion annual SDG financing gap. United States decided to exit the process entirely. New financing mechanisms announced: Debt-for-Development Swap Programme, Debt Swaps for Development Hub (led by Spain and the World Bank), Blended finance platform SCALED, Coalition for Global Solidarity Levies (led by France, Kenya and Barbados) aims to tax premium-class flying and private jets.
evidenceBullets: The Sevilla Commitment was adopted at the 4th International Conference on Financing for Development (FFD4) to close the $4 trillion annual SDG financing gap.
The United States exited the Sevilla Commitment process entirely.
Debt-for-Development Swap Programme cancels or reduces debt in exchange for government spending on development goals.
SCALED is a blended finance platform combining public, philanthropic, and private capital for sustainable development.
Coalition for Global Solidarity Levies (led by France, Kenya, Barbados) aims to tax premium-class flying and private jets for climate action.
Debt Swaps for Development Hub is led by Spain and the World Bank to scale up debt swaps and lower debt service burdens.
